content:
# Ruth Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 880–881 | **Source Image:** RUT_002_page_001.jpg, RUT_002_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Now Naomi had a relative on her husband's side, a man of considerable standing from Elimelech's clan, whose name was Boaz.

**2** Ruth the Moabite said to Naomi, "Let me go out to the fields and gather leftover grain behind anyone who will allow me." Naomi told her, "Go ahead, my daughter."

**3** So she went out and began gleaning in a field behind the harvesters. As it happened, she found herself in the portion of field belonging to Boaz, who was from Elimelech's clan.

**4** Just then, Boaz arrived from Bethlehem and greeted his workers: "The LORD be with you!" They answered him, "The LORD bless you!"

**5** Boaz asked his foreman — the one in charge of the harvesters — "Who is that young woman?"

**6** The foreman replied, "She is the Moabite girl who came back with Naomi from the country of Moab.

**7** She asked, 'Please let me glean and gather among the sheaves behind the harvesters.' She came out early this morning and has been on her feet ever since, with barely a moment's rest in the shelter."

**8** Then Boaz said to Ruth, "Listen to me, my daughter. Don't go gleaning in any other field. Don't leave this one. Stay close to my young women.

**9** Keep your eyes on whatever field they are harvesting and follow behind them. I have ordered the young men not to bother you. And whenever you are thirsty, go and drink from the water jars that the servants have filled."

**10** Ruth fell face down, bowing to the ground. She said to him, "Why have I found such favor in your eyes? Why would you take notice of me — a foreigner?"

**11** Boaz answered her, "I have been told everything you have done for your mother-in-law since your husband died — how you left your father, your mother, and the land where you were born, and came to live among a people you did not know before.

**12** May the LORD repay you fully for what you have done. May you receive a rich reward from the LORD, the God of Israel, under whose wings you have come to take shelter."

**13** She said, "You are so kind to me, my lord. You have comforted me and spoken to the heart of your servant — though I am not even one of your servant girls."

**14** At mealtime, Boaz said to her, "Come over here. Have some bread, and dip it in the vinegar sauce." So she sat beside the harvesters, and he offered her roasted grain. She ate until she was satisfied and still had some left over.

**15** When she got up to glean again, Boaz quietly instructed his young men, "Let her gather even among the sheaves — don't humiliate her.

**16** In fact, pull out some stalks from the bundles on purpose and leave them for her to pick up. And don't say a word to her about it."

**17** So Ruth gleaned in the field until evening. When she threshed out what she had gathered, it came to about an ephah of barley — an extraordinary amount.

**18** She carried it back to town, and her mother-in-law saw how much she had collected. Then Ruth brought out the leftover food from lunch and gave it to her.

**19** Naomi asked her, "Where did you glean today? Where did you work? Blessed be the man who took notice of you!" So Ruth told her about the man she had worked with. "The name of the man I worked with today," she said, "is Boaz."

**20** Naomi said to her daughter-in-law, "May the LORD bless him! He has not abandoned his loyal love — neither toward the living nor the dead." Then Naomi told her, "That man is a close relative of ours. He is one of our family redeemers."

**21** Ruth the Moabite added, "He even told me, 'Stay close to my workers until they have finished all my harvest.'"

**22** Naomi said to Ruth, her daughter-in-law, "It is good, my daughter, that you go out with his young women, so that no one bothers you in another field."

**23** So Ruth stayed close to Boaz's young women, gleaning until the end of the barley harvest and the wheat harvest. And she lived with her mother-in-law.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "a man of considerable standing":** The Hebrew אִישׁ גִּבּוֹר חַיִל describes Boaz as a man of wealth, valor, and social prominence. The phrase sets up the contrast: Ruth is a destitute foreign widow; Boaz is a pillar of the community.

**Verse 3 — "As it happened":** The Hebrew וַיִּקֶר מִקְרֶהָ literally means "her chance chanced upon." The narrator winks at the reader — what appears to be coincidence is providence at work. The understated phrasing is preserved dynamically.

**Verse 10 — "a foreigner":** Ruth uses נָכְרִיָּה (nokriyah), deliberately choosing the word for a total outsider rather than the more legally protected גֵּר (ger, resident alien). She knows her place in Israelite society and is surprised to receive kindness despite it.

**Verse 12 — "under whose wings":** The Hebrew כְּנָפָיו (kenafav) — "his wings" — pictures God as a mother bird sheltering her young. This image will return with powerful double meaning in chapter 3, when Ruth asks Boaz to spread his "wing" (same word, כָּנָף) over her.

**Verse 17 — "about an ephah":** An ephah is roughly 22 liters or about 30 pounds of grain — far more than a typical day's gleaning. The narrator emphasizes Boaz's extraordinary generosity and Ruth's tireless work.

**Verse 20 — "family redeemers":** The term גֹּאֵל (go'el) refers to the kinsman-redeemer — a close relative with the right and duty to reclaim family property and protect vulnerable family members. This is the legal and theological hinge of the entire story.

— PNEUMA
